title: Listening - Part 3: Museums in the Digital Age
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a museum curator, Elliot, and a digital artist, Libby, about how technology is changing the gallery experience. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the use of AR in galleries, the debate over 'originality' versus 'digital copies,' and making art more accessible.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Are museums 'temples of the past' or 'playgrounds for the future'? Joining me are Elliot, a curator at a national gallery, and Libby, who creates immersive digital art. Elliot, is the traditional museum under threat?

Elliot: [15] There’s a fear that if everything is available online, people won't visit. But I see the opposite. Digital access acts as a 'teaser.' [/15] People see a painting on Instagram and they want to see the 'real thing'—to stand in front of the physical object and feel its scale and history. The challenge isn't the internet; it’s making the physical space feel relevant to a younger audience.

Interviewer: Libby, you use Augmented Reality (AR) in your exhibitions. How does that change the viewer’s role?

Libby: [16] It turns the viewer from a 'passive observer' into an 'active participant.' [/16] With AR, you can point your phone at a Roman statue and see what it looked like when it was painted in bright colors 2,000 years ago. It adds layers of storytelling that a static label on a wall simply can't provide. It makes history feel alive and 'customizable.'

Interviewer: Elliot, some critics say these high-tech displays are just 'distractions' from the art itself. What do you say?

Elliot: [17] There is a danger of 'spectacle over substance.' If the technology is too flashy, people remember the 'cool effect' but forget the artist. [/17] A good curator uses technology like a spice—it should enhance the flavor of the art, not overwhelm it. The goal should always be to deepen the connection between the visitor and the object.

Interviewer: Libby, what about the idea of 'originality'? If I can see a perfect 3D projection of a sculpture, does the original still matter?

Libby: [18] I think 'aura' is still important. Walter Benjamin talked about the 'aura' of an original work of art—the sense of its unique existence in time and space. [/18] A digital copy can be perfect, but it doesn't have the 'soul' of something that was touched by the artist's hand hundreds of years ago. We don't want to replace originals; we want to give them a wider audience.

Interviewer: Elliot, museums are often accused of being 'elitist.' Can technology help with accessibility?

Elliot: [19] Immensely. For people with disabilities, or those who live in remote areas, digital tours are a lifeline. [/19] We are also using 'haptic' technology—gloves that allow blind visitors to 'feel' the texture of a sculpture through digital sensors. It’s about democratizing culture.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Libby? What will the museum of 2050 look like?

Libby: [20] It won't have walls. It will be a 'hybrid space' where the city itself becomes a gallery, with digital art projected onto buildings and history layered over every street corner. [/20]

Interviewer: Elliot, Libby, thank you.

questions.question: Elliot believes that seeing art online
options: A) makes people less interested in visiting a physical museum.
B) encourages people to see the actual object in person.
C) is a better experience than seeing the work in a gallery.
D) should be restricted to protect the artist's copyright.
questions.explanation: He says digital access acts as a 'teaser' and makes people want to see the 'real thing'.

questions.question: According to Libby, Augmented Reality (AR) allows visitors to
options: A) take better quality photographs of the exhibits.
B) interact with and learn from layers of digital story-telling.
C) change the actual physical appearance of the statues.
D) listen to music while they are walking around the gallery.
questions.explanation: She mentions it adds 'layers of storytelling' and makes history 'customizable'.

questions.question: Elliot warns that if technology in museums is 'too flashy'
options: A) the cost of tickets will become too high for most people.
B) visitors will focus on the effects rather than the art.
C) the digital devices will damage the original paintings.
D) younger audiences will become bored very quickly.
questions.explanation: He mentions the danger of 'spectacle over substance' where people forget the artist.

questions.question: Libby uses the concept of 'aura' to explain why
options: A) digital copies are actually superior to original works.
B) artists should only use traditional methods to create art.
C) an original object has a unique value that cannot be copied.
D) 3D projections are the most important part of a museum.
questions.explanation: She refers to the 'soul' and 'unique existence' of something touched by the artist's hand.

questions.question: Elliot mentions 'haptic technology' as a way to help
options: A) children play games while their parents look at the art.
B) blind visitors experience the texture of sculptures.
C) people who live in remote areas visit the museum virtually.
D) the museum save money on expensive security systems.
questions.explanation: He explains that special gloves allow blind visitors to 'feel' the art through sensors.

questions.question: Libby predicts that future museums will
options: A) be much larger buildings than those we have today.
B) only exist in the virtual world of the internet.
C) incorporate art into the everyday physical environment of the city.
D) stop showing any objects that are more than fifty years old.
questions.explanation: She envisions a 'hybrid space' where the city itself becomes the gallery.
